summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

What is a 'histogram'?

A histogram uses adjacent bars to show the frequency (count) of data values within consecutive, non-overlapping intervals.

What is a 'frequency' in the context of data distributions?

Frequency is the count of how many times a particular value or values within an interval appear in a data set.

A doctor collected the ages of 20 patients visiting a clinic in one day: 2, 5, 8, 12, 15, 22, 25, 28, 30, 35, 62, 65, 67, 68, 70, 71, 72, 74, 75, 78. What is the best description of this distribution?

The data clusters into two groups (young patients under 35 and older patients over 60) with a gap in between, making this a bimodal distribution.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Real-World Applications

Example: Which distribution shape would you expect for the ages of students in a single grade level?

Students in a single grade are roughly the same age, so their ages would cluster symmetrically around a central value.

Example: A doctor collected the ages of 20 patients visiting a clinic in one day: 2, 5, 8, 12, 15, 22, 25, 28, 30, 35, 62, 65, 67, 68, 70, 71, 72, 74, 75, 78. What is the best description of this distribution?

The data clusters into two groups (young patients under 35 and older patients over 60) with a gap in between, making this a bimodal distribution.

Example: A company surveyed employees about their commute times in minutes: most employees reported 10-30 minutes, but a few reported 60-90 minutes. The mean commute was 35 minutes and the median was 25 minutes. What does this suggest about the distribution?

Since the mean (35) is greater than the median (25), the distribution is skewed right. The few employees with very long commutes pull the mean higher than the median.

Example: A teacher gave a test and most students scored between 85 and 100, but a few students scored between 40 and 55. The mean was 78 and the median was 85. Which type of distribution does this describe?

Since the mean (78) is less than the median (85), the distribution is skewed left. The few low scores create a tail to the left, pulling the mean down.

Example: A store tracks the number of customers per hour over a 12-hour day: 15, 18, 22, 45, 52, 60, 58, 55, 40, 25, 20, 12. The store manager wants to describe the overall pattern. What shape does this distribution most likely have?

Customer counts rise from 15 to a peak around 60, then fall back to 12, creating a roughly symmetric, unimodal (single-peaked) pattern.

summary_sections.title: Special Topics
summary_sections.content_html:
Important Concepts

In a left-skewed distribution, which relationship is typically true

In a left-skewed distribution, the tail extends to the left pulling the mean lower, so the mean is typically less than the median.

In a right-skewed distribution, which relationship is typically true

In a right-skewed distribution, the tail extends toward higher values, pulling the mean to the right, so mean > median.

What does 'skewed' mean when describing a distribution

A skewed distribution is asymmetric, with data bunched on one side and a longer tail stretching toward the other side.

What is a 'frequency' in the context of data distributions

Frequency is the count of how many times a particular value or values within an interval appear in a data set.

A toy factory checks the weight of stuffed animals. Weights should be normally distributed around 200 grams. Quality control finds the distribution is skewed right. What might this indicate

A right-skewed distribution indicates some unusually heavy items pulling the tail right, suggesting some stuffed animals are being overfilled beyond the target weight.

A meteorologist collected daily high temperatures for a city over a year. The histogram shows two peaks: one around 35°F and another around 80°F. What does this bimodal distribution likely represent

A bimodal temperature distribution with peaks at 35°F and 80°F likely reflects the seasonal variation between cold winter months and warm summer months.
